key_metrics: Cash and short-term U.S. Treasury Bills combined reached a massive $339.8 billion
Investment gains declined sharply to $6.36 billion from $23.86 billion YoY
Equity method earnings swung to a $4.75 billion loss compared to a $252 million gain YoY
Insurance premiums earned remained stable at $22.20 billion vs $21.95 billion YoY
qoq_changes: Cash and cash equivalents more than doubled to $96.19 billion from $44.33 billion at year-end 2024
Short-term investments in U.S. Treasury Bills decreased to $243.61 billion from $286.47 billion at year-end 2024
Total assets increased to $1.164 trillion from $1.154 trillion at year-end 2024
new_risks: A substantial $4.75 billion loss in equity method investments significantly dragged down pre-tax earnings
Opportunity cost and inflation drag associated with hoarding nearly $340 billion in cash and short-term treasuries
Continued high volatility in headline net income due to fluctuations in unrealized investment gains
management_tone: Cautious; the continued accumulation of a record $339.8 billion in cash and T-bills strongly suggests management sees a lack of attractively valued acquisition or equity investment opportunities in the current market.
analyst_note: While Berkshire's core operating businesses like insurance and freight rail remain highly stable, headline earnings were heavily compressed by a steep drop in unrealized investment gains and a notable $4.7 billion equity method loss. The defining takeaway is the fortress balance sheet, with cash and short-term treasuries nearing $340 billion, signaling extreme patience and a highly defensive posture regarding current market valuations.
